data.title: Five Below is downgraded at Jefferies on a valuation call
data.description:
Jefferies downgraded Five Below (FIVE) to a Hold rating from Buy on a call tied to valuation. While the retailer's recent momentum was tied to CEO Winnie Park's leadership and refined merchandising strategy, tougher comparisons in the year ahead are expected to set a high bar. 

Analyst Randal Konik said with Five Below's (FIVE) valuation now well above its three-year average, the firm sees room for multiple compression as comparable sales growth normalizes back toward the long-term algorithm. He highlighted that Five Below (FIVE) has delivered comparable sales marks of 7.1%, 12.4%, and 14.3% through the first three quarters and expects 14.5% in Q4.

"This performance led management to raise its full-year comp outlook to 12.5% compared with the prior 3 to 5% range earlier this year. A meaningful portion of this momentum has been driven by ticket gains, which increased 3.4% in the second quarter and 7.2% in the third quarter, supported by $5-and-up items. As a result, the company faces a tougher setup in F’26 as it will be lapping double-digit, ticket-driven comp growth, while historically results have leaned more on traffic and transactions, making sustained momentum harder to achieve," warned Konik.

Jefferies set a price target of $210 on Five Below (FIVE).

Shares of Five Below (FIVE) were down 1.8% in premarket trading.

data.title: Earnings Snapshot: JPMorgan Chase tops Q4 estimates; investment banking revenue misses
Earnings Snapshot: Bank of New York Mellon posts Q4 double-beat
Argenx bid for Vyvgart label expansion undergoes FDA priority review
data.description:
Argenx (ARGX) announced on Tuesday that the U.S. FDA has granted priority review for its marketing application aimed at widening the approvability of Vyvgart, its blockbuster treatment for a rare autoimmune disease known as generalized myasthenia gravis.

The company stated that the regulator has accepted its supplemental Biologics License Application for Vyvgart, targeting adults with acetylcholine receptor antibody (AChR-Ab)- negative generalized myasthenia gravis ((gMG)). The FDA has issued May 10, 2026, as the target action date.

The sBLA is supported by data from Argenx’s (ARGX) Phase 3 ADAPT SERON study, which met the primary endpoint related to a gMG clinical measure over four weeks.

Vyvgart is already available in the U.S. for adults with gMG who are anti-acetylcholine receptor ((AChR)) antibody positive. The neonatal Fc receptor ((FcRn)) blocker generated $2.2B in global product net sales for the Dutch drugmaker in 2024.

data.title: Earnings Snapshot: Delta Air Lines Q4 revenue beats, FY profit guidance lags estimates
Delta falls as investors digest earnings and Dreamliner news
data.description:
Delta Air Lines (DAL) slipped in premarket trading on Tuesday after reporting Q4 results and setting profit guidance.

The airline company reported operating revenue of $16.0B, operating income of $1.5B with an operating margin of 9.2%, pre-tax income of $1.5B with a pre-tax margin of 9.5%, and earnings per share of $1.55 for the quarter vs. $1.55 consensus and $1.55 a year ago.

During the quarter, Delta (DAL) grew revenue by 1.2% year-over-year on 1.3% capacity growth and flat adjusted total unit revenue. Revenue growth was impacted by about 2 points from the government shutdown, largely in the domestic segment, consistent with the company's disclosure in early December. International performance improved significantly from Q3, with year-over-year unit revenue growth improving 5 points, driven by the transatlantic and Pacific segments. Corporate sales improved across all sectors.

"2026 is off to a strong start with top-line growth accelerating on consumer and corporate demand.  For the full year, we expect to deliver margin expansion and earnings growth of 20 percent year-over-year," stated CEO Ed Bastian.

Looking ahead, Delta (DAL) guided for 2026 EPS of $6.50 to $7.50 (midpoint $7.00) vs. $7.20 consensus. "We expect another year of cost performance aligned to our long-term framework on capacity growth of approximately 3 percent, as we continue to drive efficiencies while investing in our people and the customer experience," highlighted CFO Dan Janki.

Delta (DAL) also announced that it will add 30 new Boeing (BA) 787 Dreamliner widebody aircraft as it continues to refresh its fleet. The 787 will be a new fleet type for Delta (DAL) and complements a previous order for 100 Boeing 737-10 MAX aircraft (deliveries to begin upon certification). The 787s were noted to have wide, spacious, and quiet cabins with large, tint-controlled windows and spacious overhead storage bins that provide customers a premium experience on international flights. The aircraft was also noted to be able to climb to higher altitudes, offering smoother flights. 

Shares of Delta (DAL) fell 5.9% in premarket trading. American Airlines (AAL) dropped 2.7% and United Airlines (UAL) shed 2.5%. Southwest Airlines (LUV) was off 1.3%, while JetBlue Airways (JBLU) tracked 0.3% higher. Alaska Air Group (ALK) was down 2.2%.

data.title: Iran claims control after bloody crackdown. How will the U.S. respond?
data.description:
Security forces have "full control" of the Islamic Republic, according to Iran's Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi, following two weeks of protests and unrest. Reports suggest otherwise, but it is hard to discern information with an internet blackout and other communications largely cut off from the outside world. Various death toll figures of civilians have also been cited, ranging from several hundred to at least 12,000.

What's next: President Trump is still weighing a response that can include military strikes or cyberattacks, but in the meantime, he has opened the channel for diplomacy while hitting the country economically. "Effective immediately, any Country doing business with the Islamic Republic of Iran will pay a Tariff of 25% on any and all business being done with the United States of America," he wrote on Truth Social. "This Order is final and conclusive. Thank you for your attention to this matter!" See Iran's top trading partners

Oil is another big risk if Supreme Leader Khamenei decides to disrupt traffic in the Strait of Hormuz, and may also explain recent U.S. moves in Venezuela that can help shore up supply. About a quarter of the world’s seaborne oil passes through the critical chokepoint, as well as significant LNG exports. Any action brings its own risks, given all of the parties involved, and whether that will ultimately be enough to address Iran's currency crisis and economic mismanagement that triggered the protests in the first place.

Thought bubble: While some kind of military action is likely, President Trump doesn't necessarily want regime change, but rather a regime that will be submissive to America's global priorities and leadership. In fact, it's a playbook that's already in place even for allies, with Trump preferring administrations where he can make demands from a "stronger" place of power, rather than coming to the table from a "weaker" place of shared values. It also helps understand why he wants current governments, like the ones in Venezuela, Cuba, and Syria, to remain intact. That can all change in Iran if the population successfully fights for new leadership, but unless there is broad and visible support, don't expect Trump to go all in on "failed" regime change.
